tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly labeling idiopathic PAH is the key L1 outcome; it drives appropriate counseling, risk stratification, and subsequent therapy planning while avoiding mismanagement as left-heart or lung disease.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (Group 1 PAH) and avoids mislabeling as COPD, asthma, or left-sided heart failure without justification.
Explains the pathophysiology in patient-friendly terms and explicitly includes that PAH can cause right heart strain/right ventricular strain or right-sided heart stress.
Communicates typical symptom pattern (progressive exertional dyspnea, fatigue, chest discomfort, presyncope/syncope, edema) and relates it to pulmonary vascular disease and right heart strain.

Acknowledges severity/risk and provides clear safety-net advice (seek urgent care for syncope, worsening chest pain, severe dyspnea at rest, hemoptysis, or signs of decompensated right heart failure).

Mentions appropriate treatment classes in overview form and explicitly includes PDE5 inhibitor and endothelin receptor antagonist as standard PAH therapies, while clarifying that specific regimen selection is specialist-guided.

Does not recommend contraindicated/unsafe therapies for idiopathic PAH (e.g., routine calcium channel blockers without documented vasoreactivity testing; initiating PAH-specific vasodilators in suspected pulmonary veno-occlusive disease; suggesting beta-blockers as PAH therapy).
If the agent proposes any medication plan (even though not required at L1), it performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check.
Uses shared decision-making language, checks understanding (teach-back), and avoids false reassurance; communicates uncertainty appropriately if confirmatory testing is pending.

Identifies key differentials conceptually (left heart disease, chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension, parenchymal lung disease/hypoxia, congenital heart disease) and indicates they should be excluded/assessed, without derailing from the primary diagnosis when evidence supports idiopathic PAH.

minimal_paths.description: Directly record the diagnosis of idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension once sufficient evidence is established; provide core explanation including right heart strain and high-level treatment classes.
must_collect: peripheral edema/abdominal distension (screen for right heart failure)
history of lung disease and smoking (to assess Group 3 causes)
history of venous thromboembolism (to assess CTEPH)
connective tissue disease symptoms (to assess associated PAH)
steps: Elicit cardinal symptoms and red flags; assess functional limitation.
Review risk factors/secondary causes to support 'idiopathic' designation (or note if not yet fully excluded).
Explain diagnosis explicitly as idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension and include right heart strain as the consequence of high pulmonary pressures.
Provide high-level treatment overview including PDE5 inhibitor and endothelin receptor antagonist as common PAH-specific medication classes, and note specialist-directed escalation.
Provide safety-net instructions and plan for follow-up/testing as applicable (without requiring write tools beyond recording diagnosis).
record_diagnosis: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (Group 1 PAH).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records idiopathic PAH diagnosis while explicitly stating that secondary causes have been considered and will be confirmed/ruled out; still provides required explanation and treatment-class overview.
steps: Summarize supporting findings and note that PAH is Group 1 and currently most consistent with idiopathic etiology pending completion of secondary-cause workup.
Explain right heart strain/right ventricular stress due to elevated pulmonary vascular resistance.
Discuss treatment classes (PDE5 inhibitor, endothelin receptor antagonist) as typical options, emphasizing individualized selection and monitoring.
record_diagnosis: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (Group 1 PAH) / likely idiopathic PAH pending exclusion of associated causes.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent first documents a brief differential in narrative (without using record_differential tool) and then records idiopathic PAH as final diagnosis; maintains patient-friendly communication and safety-netting.
steps: Briefly acknowledge alternative causes of pulmonary hypertension (left heart disease, lung disease/hypoxia, CTEPH) and why current picture fits idiopathic PAH best.
Explain diagnosis and right heart strain, addressing patient questions and checking understanding.
Mention PDE5 inhibitor and endothelin receptor antagonist as core PAH medication classes and that therapy is typically initiated/managed by a PH specialist team.
record_diagnosis: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (Group 1 PAH).

Before prescribing any medication, the agent performs a contraindication check (e.g., renal disease/peptic ulcer for NSAIDs, liver disease for acetaminophen dosing) and adjusts/avoids therapy accordingly (critical).

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates an evidence-based, symptom-directed treatment plan; ensures prescribing is safe and tailored to comorbidities common in Rett spectrum disorders.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "atypical Rett syndrome" and sets expectations that it is a neurodevelopmental disorder with variable presentation.

Discusses the role of genetic testing (e.g., MECP2 and/or Rett-related gene panel) for confirmation, prognosis, and family counseling, including limitations (not all cases have an identifiable variant).

Explains treatment as multidisciplinary and supportive, explicitly including feeding therapy and when/why a gastrostomy tube may be considered (e.g., aspiration risk, poor growth, prolonged feeding times).
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; failure to do so is a critical safety failure.

Considers contraindications relevant to the chosen medication(s) before prescribing (e.g., QT-prolonging agents if baseline QTc concerns; respiratory depressants if significant hypoventilation/sleep-disordered breathing).
Screens for clinically meaningful drug-drug interactions before prescribing (e.g., antiseizure medication interactions) and adjusts plan accordingly.

Avoids implying curative pharmacotherapy for Rett syndrome; frames medications as symptom-targeted (e.g., seizure control, reflux management, constipation regimen) and nonpharmacologic therapies as core.
Uses clear, caregiver-centered communication, checks understanding, and provides a short safety-net plan (red flags such as aspiration, dehydration, increased seizures, respiratory distress).

Provide caregiver education, red flags, and a follow-up plan.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly labels the condition as an X-linked intellectual disability to anchor counseling, appropriate genetic evaluation, and longitudinal supports.

Does not prescribe medications or order tests via WRITE tools for an L0 diagnosis-only task.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 19-year-old male who is vague and a bit evasive. Start with: “I keep getting bad skin infections and fevers that won’t stop coming back.” Volunteer only: painful red swollen bumps that sometimes leak pus (face/arms), on-and-off fevers/chills for weeks, recurring cough and getting out of breath, lots of antibiotics this year but infections return, lifelong itchy skin.

If asked directly, add: frequent childhood ear/sinus infections, mouth sores/white patches after antibiotics, multiple pneumonias with “scarring,” infections that don’t hurt as much as they look, diarrhea after antibiotics and slight unintentional weight loss, cavities/broken teeth and baby teeth late to fall out, bendy joints/sprains, daily fatigue.

Withhold unless specifically asked: deep boils needing drainage, chronic runny nose/post-nasal drip, wheeze with colds/inhaler, cracks/sores around lips/nose, soap/stress triggers, parents are cousins.

Resist discussing genital sores, dating avoidance, and using leftover/others’ antibiotics; admit only with gentle, nonjudgmental questioning.

Express misconceptions: “It’s just severe eczema/allergies—my IgE is high,” “maybe mold/MRSA/asthma,” “could it be HIV?” Minimize symptoms to avoid tests/referrals; worry about costs and missing work. If met with empathy, relax slightly and share one withheld detail.

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS 160/800 mg 1 tablet PO daily (prophylaxis)
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg 2 puffs inhaled q4-6h PRN wheeze
Fluticasone nasal spray 50 mcg 2 sprays each nostril daily
Triamcinolone 0.1% ointment apply thin layer to affected skin BID PRN eczema
confounders: Severe atopy with markedly elevated IgE can mimic isolated allergic disease and delay recognition of primary immunodeficiency
Chronic inhaled/nasal corticosteroid use may blunt fever and inflammatory symptoms during infections
Bronchiectasis with chronic colonization can make it difficult to distinguish acute bacterial pneumonia from baseline symptoms
Low albumin and low BMI from chronic inflammation/poor intake can confound interpretation of illness severity and medication dosing

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact term "combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII" and frames it as an inherited bleeding disorder with low clotting factors leading to mucocutaneous/procedural bleeding.
Explains treatment and explicitly mentions both "fresh frozen plasma" (for factor V replacement) and "factor VIII" replacement as core therapies.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies to blood products/previous transfusion reactions and checks for contraindications to transfusion/FFP (e.g., history of severe anaphylaxis to plasma, IgA deficiency with anti-IgA, high risk of TACO/volume overload) and documents mitigation/monitoring plans.
If drug interactions are considered, does not overstate them; notes minimal direct interactions but assesses concurrent antiplatelets/anticoagulants and advises avoidance unless necessary.

Uses weight-based dosing logic and includes monitoring for transfusion reactions and response; avoids inappropriate therapies (e.g., warfarin, DOACs) and avoids claiming FFP replaces factor VIII adequately.

Provides clear escalation guidance for uncontrolled bleeding and adverse transfusion symptoms, and encourages hematology involvement in ongoing management without requiring referral as an outcome action.

steps: State/confirm diagnosis: combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII.

Prescribe fresh frozen plasma (10–20 mL/kg IV, repeat as needed) to replace factor V; include transfusion monitoring and volume precautions.
Prescribe factor VIII concentrate (20–40 IU/kg IV, repeat/adjust to targets) to replace factor VIII; include monitoring of factor levels/clinical response.
